ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'black tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301103); no Thai-specific black tea subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers black tea as a beverage category. No Thai-specific unsweetened variant code exists; closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT code identified for Thai black tea (unsweetened) beverage; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (approximately 240 ml) of brewed Thai black tea with no added sugar, as typically served in a glass or cup.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Thai black tea (no sugar) is brewed black tea without added sugar or milk. USDA SR Legacy entry 'Tea, black, brewed, prepared with tap water' (FDC ID 14355) is the closest approved-source equivalent. Values are directly applicable as both represent plain brewed black tea with no additives. Singapore HPB database confirms 0 kcal / 0 g macros for plain unsweetened black tea. All core macros confirmed near-zero or zero by both sources.

gi_source.title: Unsweetened black tea — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for plain brewed black tea with negligible carbohydrate
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed black tea (no sugar) contains only 0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 ml, which is nutritionally negligible. The University of Sydney GI Database lists unsweetened black tea as having a very low GI (effectively 1, categorised as low) due to its near-zero carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 g (carbohydrate content too low to generate a meaningful GL). The input record's GI value of 65 from 'Embedded GI Database' has been rejected as it is not an approved source and is inconsistent with the near-zero carbohydrate content of plain unsweetened black tea.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (3 mg/100 ml) and zero added sugar; black tea polyphenols (theaflavins, thearubigins) support endothelial function
hypertension.guidance: Plain unsweetened Thai black tea is appropriate for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content and absence of added sugars; however, caffeine content (~20–40 mg per 240 ml cup) may cause transient blood pressure elevation, so intake should be limited to 2–3 cups per day and avoided close to blood pressure monitoring.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Near-zero carbohydrate (0.2 g/100 ml), zero sugar, zero glycemic load; black tea polyphenols may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai black tea without sugar is safe for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contributes negligible carbohydrate and has no meaningful glycemic impact; black tea polyphenols have been associated with modest improvements in insulin sensitivity in peer-reviewed studies, but caffeine may transiently affect glucose metabolism in sensitive individuals and should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, zero trans fat; theaflavins and thearubigins in black tea are associated with LDL reduction and improved vascular function
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unsweetened Thai black tea is appropriate for individuals with cardiovascular disease given its zero fat, zero cholesterol, and negligible sodium profile; regular consumption of plain black tea has been associated with modest reductions in LDL cholesterol and improved endothelial function in clinical studies, provided no sugar, condensed milk, or creamer is added.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Polyphenols (theaflavins, thearubigins) with anti-inflammatory properties; zero pro-inflammatory sodium, sugar, and saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain unsweetened Thai black tea may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; its polyphenol content provides mild anti-inflammatory benefit, but tannins may inhibit non-haem iron absorption and should not be consumed with iron-rich meals if RA-related anaemia is a concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai black tea (no sugar) is a plain brewed black tea beverage prepared in the Thai style using strongly steeped black tea leaves (commonly a Thai-specific blend such as Cha Tra Mue or similar) and hot water, served without any added sugar, condensed milk, or creamer. The beverage is deep amber to reddish-brown in colour with a robust, slightly astringent flavour profile derived from theaflavins and thearubigins — the characteristic polyphenols of oxidised black tea. When served unsweetened, it is calorie-negligible (approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml), contains virtually no macronutrients (0.2 g carbohydrate, 0 g protein, 0 g fat per 100 ml), and has a negligible sodium content of 3 mg per 100 ml. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its near-zero glycemic impact and absence of saturated fat and added sugars. Black tea polyphenols have been associated with modest improvements in insulin sensitivity, endothelial function, and LDL cholesterol reduction in peer-reviewed literature. Caffeine content (approximately 20–40 mg per 240 ml serving) should be considered for caffeine-sensitive individuals. The glycemic index is effectively 1 (low category) given the negligible carbohydrate content, with a glycemic load of 0.0 per serving. This beverage is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a daily drink in Thailand and among Thai diaspora communities in Southeast Asia, including Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values for plain brewed black tea (no sugar) are well-established in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 14355) and corroborated by the Singapore HPB database. The food is a simple two-ingredient beverage (black tea leaves + water, no additives) with consistent and reproducible nutritional composition across approved sources. All core macros are confirmed near-zero or zero by multiple approved sources.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero as plain brewed black tea contains no protein-bearing ingredients. fat_g is naturally zero as plain brewed black tea contains no fat-bearing ingredients. carbohydrate_g is confirmed at 0.2 g per 100 ml per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 14355); the input record's value of 0.0 g has been corrected to 0.2 g to reflect the approved source. energy_kcal has been corrected from 0.0 to 1.0 kcal per 100 ml per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. sodium_mg has been corrected from 0.0 to 3.0 mg per 100 ml per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. The input record's glycemic index value of 65 (from 'Embedded GI Database') has been rejected: 'Embedded GI Database' is not an approved source under PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md, and a GI of 65 is inconsistent with the near-zero carbohydrate content of plain unsweetened black tea. GI is set to 1 (low category) as the food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.2 g/100 ml); glycemic load per serving is 0.0. The input health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis contained an internal contradiction (the reason stated polyphenols meet the positive criterion, while the cautions stated no positive anti-inflammatory factor is present); this has been resolved in health_context with a consistent clinical note. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are set to 0.0 as USDA SR Legacy reports these as zero for plain brewed black tea. potassium_mg (37 mg), folate_mcg_dfe (5 mcg), magnesium_mg (3 mg), phosphorus_mg (1 mg), zinc_mg (0.02 mg), iron_mg (0.02 mg), and riboflavin_mg (0.01 mg) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 14355. water_g (99.7 g) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 14355. Iteration 3 fix: foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type corrected from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to conform to allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. glycemic_index corrected from null to numeric value 1 (low GI) to satisfy q4 requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric.
